one_line_takeaway: Membership inference attacks mostly fail on LLM pre-training data, and reported successes trace to distribution shift (e.g., temporally-later non-members) rather than genuine memorization — so the membership game needs rethinking for generative models.
core_contribution: A large-scale empirical study of whether membership inference attacks (MIAs) succeed against the pre-training data of large language models. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a systematic evaluation of five EXISTING (prior-work) MIAs against the Pythia and Pythia-dedup model suites (160M-12B params) trained on the Pile, across seven domains; (2) MIMIR, a released unified benchmark/code repository re-implementing all evaluated attacks; and (3) two explanatory analyses of why MIAs largely fail on LLMs — the combination of massive training data with near-single-epoch training, and an inherently fuzzy member/non-member boundary caused by natural n-gram overlap. It further shows that reported prior successes are attributable to an unintended distribution shift (members vs. temporally-later non-members) rather than true memorization, and argues the membership game itself needs rethinking for generative models. The five attacks themselves (LOSS, Reference-based, Zlib, Neighborhood, Min-k% Prob) are all from cited prior work, not introduced here.
threat_model: Black-box membership inference: an adversary with query access to a target LLM computes a per-sample membership score (thresholded loss or token-probability statistic) to decide whether a candidate text was in the model's pre-training corpus, as a proxy for privacy leakage/memorization. The Reference-based variant additionally requires a same-distribution reference model — which the paper finds hard to obtain at pre-training scale because independently collected web corpora overlap. The study is only feasible on open models with known training membership (Pythia/Pile), so it characterizes the difficulty of the attack rather than proposing a new one.
limitations: Stated/evident limitations: (1) The study relies on open models with known training-set membership (Pythia/Pile, GPT-Neo, Datablations, SILO); it does not establish behavior on closed frontier LLMs. (2) The authors could not reproduce the exact experimental settings of prior works reporting high MIA performance (Shi et al. 2023; Meeus et al. 2023), so it is inconclusive whether those results are due solely to temporal distribution shift. (3) The ≤20% n-gram-overlap resampling that boosts AUC deliberately drifts from the standard membership-inference game (toward distribution inference), so those higher numbers are not a recommended benchmarking practice. (4) MIA performance could improve with stronger future attacks; the negative result reflects current attacks, not a fundamental impossibility. (5) Choosing a good reference model is largely empirical and hard to satisfy at pre-training scale. (6) The 'approximate membership' analyses (lexical/semantic modified members) are exploratory initial steps, not a fully specified new membership definition.

datasets_used: Pile subsets: Pile-CC, Wikipedia, PubMed Central, ArXiv, HackerNews, DM Mathematics, GitHub
quantitative_results: No MIA/target-model AUC ROC exceeds 0.6 in any domain except GitHub (Table 1); GitHub outlier peaks ~0.717 (Pythia-dedup-2.8B)
Average 7-gram overlap of non-members vs. Pile training (§3.2.2): GitHub 76.9%, DM Math 72.8%, FreeLaw 62.3%, PubMed Central 41.0%, ArXiv 39.3%, Wikipedia 32.5%
Resampling non-members to <=20% 7-gram overlap raises AUC ROC on Pythia-dedup-12B (Table 2): GitHub .690->.908, PubMed Central .512->.792, ArXiv .527->.787, Wikipedia .516->.666
Temporally-shifted Wikipedia benchmark (Table 3) reaches AUC .796 (Reference attack, 12B); Reference AUC .602->.796 as model scales 160M->12B
Temporal shift lowers average 7-gram overlap of Wikipedia non-members from 39.3% (natural, §4) to 13.9% (shifted)

Modified members (edit distance n=1..25, or 5% semantic token swaps) yield very low FPR when treated as non-members (Table 4; LOSS attack FPR = 0 across all tested thresholds for lexical edits), i.e. they are classified as non-members despite equivalent information leakage
baselines_compared: Five existing prior-work MIAs are evaluated against each other and against random guessing (AUC ROC 0.5) as the reference point: LOSS (Yeom et al. 2018), Reference-based (Carlini et al. 2021; best overall), Zlib Entropy (Carlini et al. 2021), Neighborhood attack (Mattern et al. 2023), Min-k% Prob (Shi et al. 2023). Note: Pythia, Pythia-dedup, GPT-Neo, Datablations, and SILO are target-model families/ablations, not attack baselines.
